literal: practice of yoga; yoga (discipline) + ācāra (conduct, practice) — "the path of yogic practice"
invariant_definition: Yogācāra is the Mahāyāna Buddhist school founded by Asaṅga and Vasubandhu (c. 4th c. CE), also called Vijñānavāda ("doctrine of consciousness") or Cittamātra ("mind-only"). It holds that what we take to be external objects are not given independently of consciousness: experience is analyzed as the transformation of consciousness (vijñāna-pariṇāma), and the duality of grasper and grasped is an imagined construction. Its key structures are the store-consciousness (ālaya-vijñāna) that carries karmic seeds, the three natures (trisvabhāva) — imagined, dependent, and perfected — and the "representation-only" thesis (vijñapti-mātratā). Liberation is a turning-about at the basis (āśraya-parāvṛtti) in which the imagined duality ceases and suchness (tathatā) is realized.
